name: Coatlicue
title: Aztec - Coatlicue
symbols: Her most defining feature - a skirt made of intertwined serpents, representing the earth's fertility and dangerous power

primarySources.title: Florentine Codex
primarySources.author: Fray Bernardino de Sahagún
primarySources.text: Sahagún's Florentine Codex provides detailed descriptions of Aztec religious beliefs and practices, including accounts of Coatlicue and her role in the Aztec pantheon. Book 3, in particular, focuses on the origins of the gods.

primarySources.title: Histoyre du Mechique
primarySources.author: André Thévet
primarySources.text: Thévet's Histoyre du Mechique offers insights into Aztec history and religion, including mentions of Coatlicue and her significance as a mother goddess. While not as comprehensive as Sahagún, it provides valuable corroborating information.

primarySources.title: Anales de Cuauhtitlán
primarySources.author: Anonymous
primarySources.text: The Anales de Cuauhtitlán, part of the Codex Chimalpopoca, contains a creation myth that features Coatlicue and the birth of Huitzilopochtli. It offers a different perspective on the events surrounding Coatlicue's pregnancy and the subsequent conflict with her other children.

symbols: Her most defining feature - a skirt made of intertwined serpents, representing the earth's fertility and dangerous power

description:
Coatlicue, whose name translates to "Serpent Skirt" in Nahuatl, is a prominent figure in Aztec mythology, embodying the complex and often paradoxical nature of the earth and motherhood. She is revered as the mother of gods, including the war god Huitzilopochtli, the moon goddess Coyolxauhqui, and the Centzon Huitznahua, the four hundred southern stars. Her image and stories are deeply intertwined with themes of creation, destruction, fertility, death, and sacrifice, reflecting the cyclical worldview of the Aztec people. She is also known by the epithets Teteoinan ("Mother of Gods"), Toci ("Our Grandmother"), Cihuacoatl ("Serpent Woman"), and Tonantzin ("Our Revered Mother").

Coatlicue's origin story, as recounted in various sources including the Florentine Codex compiled by Fray Bernardino de Sahagún, centers around her miraculous pregnancy. While sweeping the temple of Coatepec (Serpent Mountain), a ball of feathers (specifically, hummingbird feathers, according to some accounts) fell upon her, impregnating her. This unexpected event angered her existing children, Coyolxauhqui and the Centzon Huitznahua, who considered it a dishonor to their mother. They plotted to kill her, viewing the pregnancy as a sign of impurity or infidelity. Their anger stemmed from the disruption of the established order and the perceived threat to their own power and status within the cosmic hierarchy.

The most famous myth associated with Coatlicue is the birth of Huitzilopochtli. As Coyolxauhqui and the Centzon Huitznahua approached Coatepec to carry out their plan, Huitzilopochtli sprang forth fully armed from his mother's womb. He immediately defended Coatlicue, slaying Coyolxauhqui and scattering the Centzon Huitznahua across the sky, transforming them into the stars. This myth is not merely a story of birth; it is a powerful allegory of the constant struggle between opposing forces: light and darkness, order and chaos, and the triumph of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the moon and stars. The dismemberment of Coyolxauhqui is often interpreted as a representation of the moon's phases, while the scattering of the Centzon Huitznahua symbolizes the dispersal of stars at dawn.

The symbolism associated with Coatlicue is rich and multifaceted. Her most striking feature, the skirt of serpents, represents the earth's fertile yet dangerous power. Serpents, in Mesoamerican cultures, were often associated with fertility, the earth, and the underworld. The fact that they form her skirt suggests the all-encompassing nature of her power, encompassing both life and death. Her necklace, made of human hearts and hands, and her skull-like face, further emphasize the connection between life, death, and sacrifice. These elements highlight the Aztec understanding of the world as a place where life is sustained by death and sacrifice is necessary to maintain cosmic balance. The clawed feet suggest her connection to the earth and her power over the terrestrial realm.

Furthermore, Coatlicue is often depicted with dual serpent heads emerging from her neck, where her head should be. This imagery is interpreted in several ways. Some scholars believe it represents the duality of her nature, encompassing both creation and destruction. Others see it as a symbol of the blood that flowed from her neck after Huitzilopochtli was born, representing the life-giving force of sacrifice. The headless depiction can also be linked to the myth of Coyolxauhqui's dismemberment, suggesting a connection between the two figures and the cyclical nature of life and death.

Coatlicue's cultural significance within Aztec society was profound. As the mother of Huitzilopochtli, the patron deity of the Aztec capital Tenochtitlan, she held a central position in the Aztec pantheon. She was venerated in various rituals and ceremonies, often involving offerings and sacrifices. Her image served as a constant reminder of the importance of sacrifice in maintaining cosmic order and ensuring the continued prosperity of the Aztec people. The temple of Coatepec, where the myth of her miraculous pregnancy took place, was a significant pilgrimage site, further highlighting her importance in Aztec religious life. The reenactment of the birth of Huitzilopochtli, where a warrior would symbolically defeat his enemies, mirrored the myth and reinforced the Aztec belief in the power of their patron deity and the necessity of warfare to sustain their empire.

Coatlicue's role as a primordial mother goddess is also significant. She represents the ultimate source of life and creation, but also the inevitability of death and decay. This duality is a key characteristic of many earth mother goddesses across different cultures. She embodies the cyclical nature of existence, where life emerges from death and death gives way to new life. This concept was central to the Aztec worldview, which emphasized the interconnectedness of all things and the importance of maintaining balance within the cosmos. Her association with serpents further reinforces this connection to the earth and the underworld, realms associated with both fertility and death.

While the primary sources provide valuable insights into Coatlicue's mythology and significance, interpretations vary among scholars. Some emphasize her role as a fearsome and powerful deity, highlighting her association with death and sacrifice. Others focus on her nurturing and life-giving aspects, emphasizing her role as a mother goddess. However, it is generally agreed that Coatlicue represents a complex and multifaceted figure, embodying the fundamental principles of the Aztec worldview. Her enduring presence in Aztec art and mythology serves as a testament to her lasting impact on the culture and beliefs of the Aztec people.

In conclusion, Coatlicue is more than just a mother goddess; she is a symbol of the earth's power, the cyclical nature of life and death, and the importance of sacrifice in maintaining cosmic balance. Her myths and imagery offer a profound glimpse into the complex religious beliefs and worldview of the Aztec civilization. The study of Coatlicue provides valuable insights into the broader themes of creation, destruction, and renewal that are central to human understanding of the world around us.

Goddess of the Sky | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of Love & Joy | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Sacrifice
worshipPractices.description: Coatlicue, as a powerful earth goddess and mother of the gods, received offerings to ensure fertility and maintain cosmic balance. These offerings often included human sacrifices, particularly of war captives, and animal sacrifices, believed to nourish the gods and ensure continued prosperity.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers for Fertility
worshipPractices.description: As the mother of Huitzilopochtli and other important deities, Coatlicue was petitioned for fertility and a successful harvest. Women would pray to her for healthy pregnancies and safe childbirth, while farmers would seek her blessing for abundant crops.

festivals.name: Panquetzaliztli
festivals.timing: Held during the Aztec month of Panquetzaliztli (November/December)
festivals.description: This festival primarily celebrated the birth of Huitzilopochtli, Coatlicue's son. It involved elaborate processions, ritual battles, and the reenactment of Huitzilopochtli's birth and triumph over his siblings, symbolizing the victory of the sun over darkness.

festivals.name: Toxcatl
festivals.timing: Held during the fifth month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: While primarily dedicated to Tezcatlipoca, some scholars believe that offerings and reverence were also given to Coatlicue during Toxcatl, acknowledging her role as a powerful earth goddess. Sacrifices, dances, and fasting were common practices.

cultural.modernLegacy: Coatlicue's image remains a powerful symbol of Aztec identity and cultural heritage. Her statue is prominently displayed in museums and continues to inspire artists, writers, and filmmakers, who explore themes of motherhood, creation, and the cyclical nature of life and death. She also appears in modern literature and art as a representation of indigenous strength and resilience.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Her name, 'Serpent Skirt', directly links her to snakes, symbolizing fertility, power, and the earth's raw energy.

associations.name: Maize
associations.significance: As an earth goddess, she is associated with maize, a staple crop representing nourishment and sustenance.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: Coatlicue is a primordial earth goddess, embodying the life-giving and life-taking aspects of the planet.

associations.name: Green
associations.significance: Represents the fertility of the earth and the abundance of nature, aligning with Coatlicue's maternal role.

associations.name: Motherhood
associations.significance: She is the mother of many important deities, embodying the role of a nurturing and powerful mother figure.

associations.name: Duality
associations.significance: Coatlicue represents the duality of life and death, creation and destruction, inherent in the natural world.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: As the mother of Coyolxauhqui (the moon goddess), she has an indirect association with the lunar cycle and its connection to fertility.

companions.name: Huitzilopochtli
companions.description: Huitzilopochtli is Coatlicue's most famous son, born miraculously after she was impregnated by a ball of feathers. He is the god of war, sun, and sacrifice, and his birth is central to Aztec mythology.

companions.name: Coyolxauhqui
companions.description: Coyolxauhqui is Coatlicue's daughter and leader of the Centzon Huitznahua. She attempted to kill her mother after learning of Huitzilopochtli's impending birth, but was defeated and dismembered.

companions.name: Centzon Huitznahua
companions.description: The Centzon Huitznahua are Coatlicue's four hundred sons, who, along with Coyolxauhqui, plotted against their mother. They represent the southern stars and were defeated by Huitzilopochtli.

companions.name: Quetzalcoatl
companions.description: Quetzalcoatl is one of Coatlicue's sons, known as the feathered serpent deity. He is associated with wisdom, wind, and learning and often appears in duality with Tezcatlipoca.

companions.name: Tezcatlipoca
companions.description: Tezcatlipoca is another of Coatlicue's powerful sons, representing the night, sorcery, and destiny. He is often seen as a rival or counterpart to Quetzalcoatl, embodying opposing forces.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Birth of Huitzilopochtli
extendedContent.content:
Coatlicue's most famous myth involves the miraculous birth of Huitzilopochtli. According to legend, while sweeping the temple of Coatepec (Serpent Mountain), a ball of feathers descended from the sky and impregnated Coatlicue. This immaculate conception angered her other children, Coyolxauhqui and the Centzon Huitznahua, who considered it a dishonorable act. They conspired to kill their mother to avenge the perceived shame. 

As Coyolxauhqui and her brothers approached Coatlicue, Huitzilopochtli sprang forth fully armed from his mother's womb. He immediately vanquished Coyolxauhqui, dismembering her body and scattering the pieces across the mountain. This event, powerfully depicted in monumental sculptures, symbolizes the triumph of the sun (Huitzilopochtli) over the moon (Coyolxauhqui) and the stars (Centzon Huitznahua), reflecting the daily struggle between light and darkness. The myth of Huitzilopochtli's birth reinforces Coatlicue's role as a powerful mother goddess capable of producing a formidable warrior deity.

Sahagún's Florentine Codex provides a primary account of this myth, detailing the resentment of Coyolxauhqui and the Centzon Huitznahua and Huitzilopochtli's swift defense of his mother. This narrative highlights the Aztec emphasis on warfare and the cyclical nature of death and rebirth, as well as the inherent conflicts within the cosmos. Coatlicue’s association with both creation and destruction is central to understanding her complex role.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation
extendedContent.content:
Coatlicue's iconography is among the most striking and complex in Aztec art. She is typically depicted as a fearsome figure, a composite of various symbols representing the earth's generative and destructive forces. Her most recognizable feature is her skirt made of interwoven serpents (hence her name), symbolizing fertility and the life-giving power of the earth. Her severed head is sometimes replaced by two serpent heads facing each other, representing the duality of life and death. 

She often wears a necklace made of human hearts, hands, and skulls, symbolizing the sacrifices necessary to sustain the cosmos. Her breasts are often depicted as deflated, signifying her role as a mother who has nourished many. Her hands and feet are adorned with claws, further emphasizing her connection to the earth and its power. The Monumental Coatlicue statue, discovered in Mexico City, is a prime example of this complex iconography. 

These gruesome elements, however, should not be interpreted solely as representations of death and destruction. They also symbolize the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, essential to the Aztec worldview. Coatlicue's appearance is not meant to be merely terrifying, but rather to convey the powerful and transformative forces of nature that she embodies, reflecting the Aztec understanding of the interconnectedness of all things.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Temples
extendedContent.content:
While dedicated temples specifically to Coatlicue are not as well-documented as those of other major deities like Huitzilopochtli or Tlaloc, her presence was undoubtedly felt in various Aztec religious practices. Shrines and altars may have been dedicated to her within larger temple complexes, particularly those associated with fertility and the earth. Offerings of food, flowers, and possibly even human sacrifices would have been made to honor her and ensure the continued bounty of the earth.

Excavations at the Templo Mayor in Mexico City have yielded significant finds related to Coatlicue, including the monumental statue described above. These discoveries suggest that Coatlicue held an important position within the state pantheon, even if she did not have a dedicated temple of the same scale as other major deities. Her presence at the Templo Mayor, the most important religious center in the Aztec capital, underscores her significance in the Aztec worldview.

Furthermore, rituals involving the earth and fertility likely incorporated elements of Coatlicue's worship. The celebration of the harvest, for instance, may have included prayers and offerings to Coatlicue as the embodiment of the earth's generative power. Her influence extended beyond specific temples, permeating various aspects of Aztec religious life and reflecting her vital role in the sustenance of the Aztec people.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
Coatlicue plays a crucial role in the Aztec understanding of cosmic order and the cyclical nature of existence. As the mother of Huitzilopochtli, Coyolxauhqui, and the Centzon Huitznahua, she is intimately connected to the celestial realm and the constant struggle between opposing forces. The myth of Huitzilopochtli's birth and victory over his siblings is a powerful allegory for the daily struggle between the sun and the moon, light and darkness, order and chaos. 

Through her role as a mother goddess, Coatlicue embodies both creation and destruction, life and death. She is the source of all life on earth, but she also demands sacrifice to maintain the cosmic balance. This duality is a fundamental aspect of the Aztec worldview, which emphasized the interconnectedness of all things and the constant need for renewal. The earth, in the Aztec understanding, is both a source of sustenance and a place of death and decay, a concept perfectly encapsulated by Coatlicue's complex iconography.

Her association with the earth and fertility links her to the agricultural cycle and the sustenance of the Aztec people. By honoring Coatlicue, the Aztecs sought to ensure the continued fertility of the land and the success of their crops. Her position within the pantheon highlights the importance of the earth and its resources in the Aztec worldview, reflecting their deep connection to the natural world and their understanding of the delicate balance that sustained their society.
